name: Zhang et al. GPT Essay Training Augmentation Extract
population_or_context: PERSUADE 2.0 essays from U.S. students in grades 6-10 across seven source-based writing prompts; each prompt is modeled separately.
sample_size: Student essay samples ranged from 1,372 to 2,167 per prompt. GPT-4 generated about 120 usable essays per score level; GPT-4o generated about 275 usable essays per score level for model training.
methods: Student essays were split into generator-feeding, linguistic-comparison/training, and held-out evaluation subsets. GPT-4 and GPT-4o generated score-conditioned essays from prompts containing rubric properties, directives, title/template information, and sampled real essay examples. DeBERTa-V3-XSmall scoring models were trained with student-only or student-plus-GPT samples and evaluated on held-out student essays.
education_context: Middle and high school source-based essay writing assessment research using a 6-point essay score scale.
response_origin: Hybrid training corpora of student-written essays and GPT-generated essays; final model evaluation uses held-out student essays.
rubric_label_space: Six-point essay score scale. The publicly released student essays have one available rater score, though the corpus reports two-rater scoring with inter-rater QWK .745 and correlation .750.
label_source: Student essay labels come from the PERSUADE 2.0 human-scored corpus. GPT-generated essays are generated for target score levels and used as training augmentation; the study cautions that target score levels are not equivalent to expert human scoring of each generated essay.
generation_method: Score-conditioned GPT generation across prompts and score levels using rubric-driven properties, custom directives, prompt templates, and sampled student essay examples.
prompt_strategy: Five-part prompt design with system prompt, custom directives, rubric-driven essay properties, title/generation template, and prompt directives. Each generation call included five sampled real essays for the target prompt and score level.
evaluation_method: Held-out student essay evaluation using human-AI QWK, correlation, standardized mean score difference, subgroup mean difference in standardized scores, subgroup QWK, linguistic-feature comparison, and DecompX token-importance analysis.
scorer_use: GPT-generated essays are used as training augmentation for prompt-specific AI essay scoring models, not as standalone validation data.
real_response_comparator: The study compares GPT-generated essays with student essays and evaluates augmented scoring models on held-out student essays that were not used during training.
reported_metrics: Most augmented and base models, except the P7 A Cowboy condition, achieved QWK and correlation above .75 on held-out student essays. SMD generally fell within +/- .15. Augmentation reduced some subgroup mean score discrepancies and improved some subgroup QWKs.
limitations: Research/development study, not an operational scoring policy or state technical report.
Only one human rating is available in the public data, so true-score evaluation was not conducted.
The authors report content and writing-style differences between AI-generated and student essays that need systematic human review.
Results are limited to GPT-4, GPT-4o, the PERSUADE 2.0 prompts, and the studied model architecture; prompt transfer to other LLMs is uncertain.
Target score levels for generated essays should not be treated as independently human-verified labels.
